speaker: interviewer
text: So you were able to bring your father's body back to Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and all other family member were killed over in the country, as usual. So my mother was, of course, survived then because she was hiding, and my brother was in Switzerland [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother have any unusual experiences while hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't think she wanted to tell me too much about it. I think that something happened with her when the Russians came, but it was somehow-- she didn't want to talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the Russian came--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians raped a lot of people and stole everything. All our furniture, they cut it in pieces, and they used it for fire and so  forth. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother and Anna--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: --are together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and then, so we went back to our home. Actually, the building that we lived at--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is an apartment building?

speaker: interviewee
text: Apartment building had a bomb hit, so that my room was actually over some big hole. And that's the way we lived. And I went to the medical school, and I wanted to be an architect, but the architect school was in Buda, and I lived in Pest, and the bridge was not easily too available. So I decided I will be a doctor. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the way it happened. Interesting how decisions are made.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think so. I didn't mind it, but it was not my first choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother came back for a very short time. My brother was always a very keen traveler. And so when he came back, he renewed his passport, and he became very important. He became, actually, the head of the Hungarian Tungsram Electrical Company-- an engineer, which is now the General Electric Company actually. And he was in a very good position. But the following days after his passport was renewed, the communists stopped giving out passports. So my brother had a valid passport for six months, and he felt that he should leave and that's the only way we're going to survive the Russians and communists. And eventually, he left. He went to Israel. My mother emigrated to Israel in 1951 and was one among the very few lucky who could go. It was not easy because it was a lot of complications, but eventually, [? you know. ?] And then, I, eventually following the Hungarian revolution, left and I had the joy of that system too.

speaker: interviewer
text: The joy?

speaker: interviewee
text: The communist system, I had the great pleasure of that one.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- how were you treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the thing is that I was always a free spirit. And in the medical school, I-- the communists started to take over. And I made a fake-- how do I say-- party, or something like that, and it's for the Fish Party. And I made a lot of fun about the communists. And they had May 1 one day-- one year, where every student had to go out to demonstrate. And they want to give me a-- some kind of things that you carry. I don't know how the word--

speaker: interviewer
text: A sign?

speaker: interviewee
text: A sign, and it was a Truman with a snake around his neck. And I said, I'm not taking this. And they said, why don't you take it? I said, there's no way that I'm going to carry a sign against the American who saved my life. And so they put it next to my body, and I step out and [INAUDIBLE], and I left. And from there on, I was excluded from any activities as a student, and eventually they forced me to go to the country to work as a general doctor, rather than a specialist. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: They did let you finish?

speaker: interviewee
text: After I finished my medical school. And then, eventuality, I get to Budapest, and the revolution in 1956-- October-- took place, and I happened to be just accidentally there when the revolution broke out in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a lot of commotion, but in the evening on October 23 I think--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is in Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest, I had a medical meeting at the university. And next to the university and the chemistry department is the radio station. And the Hungarian Prime Minister [? Hegedus ?] was supposed to give a talk. And the Hungarian students went to block the entrance of the radio station in order to prevent him to go there. Of course, they were very naive. They didn't realize there can be another way to be on the radio. They don't have to be there physically. And the secret party to started to machine gun them. And I just happened to be there. And-- and we took the injured kids to the nearest place. And I thought so that's the end of the whole thing. And I thought what a system who has to shoot innocent children with machine gun. And the next morning-- I went home. The next morning, I look out, and I see that youngsters with machine guns, because the Hungarian military was called, eventually give the weapons for them. And then, I decided-- I was working at a time in a premature hospital-- that I don't want to work with prematures, because it's ridiculous to save a premature when 16-year-olds are killed. And I went to a place, which was a hospital I was working before. And I said-- with some other people organized a system that we carried patients in and out. And it became the center of the revolution, eventually. And I was not a revolutionary, but I maintained very much the dignity of the hospital, and we saved Russians as well as anyone else. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the beginning of the revolution, this the radio station?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the match?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and then eventually, we went to Austria, brought back some food and medications. And of course, a revolution was a hopeless thing, anyhow. And people who I was working with were executed and so forth because it was-- because they said that we brought weapons back to the country. And so anyhow, so I, eventually, I left the country, and I escaped. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I lost about 9 kilograms, which is about 18-some pounds, because during this revolution, I was working with anesthesia and so forth. And then, I went to the border, and I hired someone, and they took me over. So I, eventually-- my brother at that time was in the United States, and I wasn't sure I should go Israel or United States. I came to United States, and my mother, the next year, came to United States too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a visa or passport?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. [LAUGHS] No visa, no passport-- you are a refugee, or something like that. And just two years ago, they gave me a medal for my heroic behavior in Hungary, which they just previously they wanted to execute me for. So you know, it's nice. But it's better this way. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: If I have a choice to make.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Now, in-- in this situation where you were-- you were to be shot, were you-- you with the group that were killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the group, and they stopped the shooting right there. So that we were able to see there's a lot of bodies, when the three men was arrested.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who had gone to Austria and you yourself had gone to Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you're talking about-- no, no, you're talk-- oh, I'm sorry. I thought we'd go back to something. No, no, you're talking about this. No, these people were executed by the communists later. The police was looking for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were looking for you, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. By that time, I was at the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you they were looking for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because she kept the bed. She was living in our home. She stayed with our home until she died. And I went to see her a few times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Since?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, she was a [? keeper ?] person. She could never come out. It was very hard for me to go back. I hated to go back, and I was always in a great fear to go back to these places, but I had to do it because she was there. And my father was there. My father was there, buried you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so it was to me to like to go to [INAUDIBLE]. One day, I remember, each time I went back, I went the first day and the last day in the cemetery. And one day, I went there, and the cemetery was closed. It was some kind of, not major, but holiday. You cannot go in the cemetery, you know, during this time when it's holiday. So I-- I climbed to the wall, and I went to see him. I think, that's to me, that's the reason I go back. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little bit about coming to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was very interesting. It was actually very funny, because on the-- from Salzburg, we arrived in Briemhaufen. It was the American Navy sent there. And we it the first Navy boat we came out with. Previously, the Air Force brought the Hungarians.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I go on the boat, and they page me. And there's a rabbi from Buffalo, New York who said, Dr. Fisher, I'm very glad to see you. And I want to ask you that you think it's enough to give-- do we have enough food for 100 people-- kosher food? I said that's enough for me, because I'm the only Jewish in the boat. [LAUGHS] So it turned out to be, so from there on I really had to go to pray in the morning and pray in the evening, which wasn't easy for me. And the other priests and ministers came to help too. And then, I give, of course, Mogen David wine. And some people thought I'd got a good deal going for me. Well, then very soon, a few Jews came out from nowhere. And at that time, I was very happy I didn't have to go and [LAUGHS] pray every morning and every evening. Well, anyhow, and then it was an admiral, also, on the boat-- a medical admiral-- and he said that when we get to New York in January, 1957, he said can I do a favor for you? And I said yes, call my brother. He called my brother in New York and said, this is admiral such and such in January early morning, on January 1. And my brother awakened from deep sleep and said here's [INAUDIBLE] speaking, [? you know ?] [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyhow. I came to the States. And then eventually, I met in New York City some very beautiful people who helped Hungarian refugees. And through their help, I came to Minnesota, and I started from the beginning, obviously, in 1958 June. And I have been here since.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to take new examinations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, we even had to take the high school examination.

speaker: interviewer
text: High school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. High school. [LAUGHS] I actually was best in that situation in mathematics because you don't have to speak the language. [LAUGHS] When it comes to Shakespeare, you have a trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your English was getting better and better, obviously.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my English is getting better and better. But you know what they say, the first day when you come, you speak so good English. The second year, they said you have an interesting accent. And in five years, they say you never learned the language. I'm in that stage now. And then, I was here at the University. I have been here, which is very good with me, and I became very involved in a certain kind of disease, which is called PKU, or phenylketonuria. And this is-- every new born baby is screened for it. And I got involved, in the last 30-some years, in that. And it's become nationally known, internationally, our work here. And it's a wonderful experience, because previously these children were severely, profoundly retarded, and now they go to high schools and college and have a normal existence with early detection. And I-- so it's a very rewarding thing. But I liked, you know, when I finished here on the basic groundwork, so to speak, I went back to paint. And I started to take courses at the University, at Walker, at the Minneapolis College of Art. For more than nine years, every week, I took some courses in the evening. And I started to paint and draw and so forth. And one day, I go to the which is the journal for the physicians in Minnesota and saw the editor. And I told her I would like to do some illustrations. And she called me back within two hours, and she said, I have a work for you, if you're interested, to have a design for a cover for the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the Holocaust? I mean, gosh, first of all, I'm not qualified, as far as I'm concerned, to do something like that. And the second, how come a medical journal is involved in the Holocaust? And it turned out to be there was an ethic meeting in Duluth, Minnesota. So they wanted to have an all issue about should or they should not use the Nazi experimentations for scientific purpose.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I say first, I don't know. I want to think about it. But the next day, I said, if I don't do it, someone else is doing it. But it was not difficult. I made a design.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then within a week or so, she called me up. She said, why don't you write an article? It was very difficult for me, because I wanted to not tell my story, because my story is irrelevant. But I wanted to make people feel how I feel when I go to the cemetery my father is in. And it's a memorial cemetery. He is the first one next to it, which is like the Vietnam Wall.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's just nobody buried, just names or quotations. And these quotations are probably from the Job book-- the Book of Job. And it's a very, very heavy-duty quotations. And I wanted these quotations to be part of my story. And this editor did a beautiful job and put my quotations in a marble kind of thing. And when I went back to the-- when this was published, it was very hard on me, because emotionally it really drained me. When I went back to the university, the doctors hugged me and cried, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which was very unusual behavior for a Swedish and Norwegian. And I suddenly realized that it's a way of expressing something. But I didn't want to express the horror of the whole. I wonder what we can learn. The Holocaust is something which is unique in history, by the circumstances and the way how it was carried out. But I felt I'm not a historian and not even an objective witness. I have to somehow-- what those people who are killed would like us to do. And I don't think they want us to express hate and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Holocaust is not a German-Jewish issue, as far as I'm concerned. It's a human issue. And I thought so I have an opportunity here or something. And I was invited to go to Pine City, Minnesota, which is a little place. And I thought, so why not Rome or Paris or something? In Pine City, Minnesota, I talked to a little-- a little group, for example, the first day evening.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They invited me because they read about the article. And it was a gentleman who stood up and said thank you, Dr. Fisch. You don't even know what it meant, what you said. Well, it's at that time that the bombing in Budapest became to me a very enjoyable-- not enjoyable-- but I knew it's going to help me. And he said I was one of those bombardier. Up to this point, I always felt bad that I killed innocent people. Now, I know I saved lives. And so that was the first of these kind of unusual experiences.

speaker: interviewer
text: The next day, I talked with a selected group of school kids. And it was a girl there who was very unisex looking-- I didn't even know if it was a girl or boy-- who was very intensively watching me. And when I finished with is there any question, some did. And I turned to her, don't you have any questions? At that time, I was talking about that the Danish people was the only group of people who save the Jews, because the king on everybody had the yellow star. And she said Dr. Fisch, you don't know what it meant to me what you said. I'm a Danish visiting student, and my grandmother saved a family. Now, I know what it meant. From there on, the principal told me, why didn't I write a book? And I did, which was an exhibit in addition. And that's what I've been doing now. Mention the name of your book?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's called And this is now a foundation, and we give this to children to learn from. And I'm going to Israel. And in Israel, I'm going to have a show, and we're going to a foundation to have this book in Hungary, because what I really want to say how to remain human in an inhuman circumstance.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to go back a little bit in time and mention after you came to America, you were married. Yes, I got married in-- in-- in May 30, 1969. And I have a beautiful daughter, but unfortunately, I divorced. So-- But my daughter is a lot of joy for me and my hope for the future. Is there any way in which you can begin to bring this whole experience together and make some kind of meaning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it's-- of course, I'm not representing anyone but myself. I think that it's very important to realize that a person has to stand up for morale values very early and should never sacrifice and sell away. If you do, you have two choices to make-- either become part of the suppressor or the victim. Otherwise, if you get in a situation like that, I think this is to me not German and Jewish issue. This is a human issue. And I think that everywhere is a certain population who's willing to do bad things, and a very few population is outstanding.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went back last year on the 50th anniversary to thank the soldiers who saved my life. And I came to the frightening thought, what would have happened if I would live there in the neighborhood, and I would see these people going by, what I would do. How many times I could run in and out and give some food when someone is killed giving just apple away? Would I risk myself and my family to save someone who I don't even know? It's a very difficult question.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think that Schindler's list, Schindler did something. He had a very charismatic person. He had a lot of influence, and he saved finally 1,000 people. It's too late. You have to stand up very early against oppression for your sake as well as the other. I think we, as humans, basically are biologically animals. Killing and hate and taking away is a natural thing. To be a human, you have to learn to be civilized, how to live together with someone else. I think that's what you have to do, and it's not an easy thing to do. That's my conclusion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Dr. Fisch.

speaker: interviewee
text: Welcome, and thank you very much for the opportunity to have this for me. This is very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have some pictures we want to take a look at. Tell me who this is.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandmother-- my mother's mother-- called Thereza Rottman, R-O-T-T-M-A-N. And she was the wife of my grandfather, whose name is Herman Manheim. And she is-- this is a young picture of her. It's probably in Zenta, Z-E-N-T-A, which at that time belonged to Hungary. It's currently called Senta and belongs to Yugoslavia. The-- oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are these?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are also my grandmother at an older age, of course. And her husband, Herman Manheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: They're from?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're also from Zenta, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these also are your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is my father's mother and father. The father name is-- my grandfather is named Markus, and that's his wife, who was Betti Berger. And this picture was taken in Budapest in 1919. Your shadow is there. This is my father before wedding. He was probably about 20 years old in Budapest. My father is all done. Do you want to try the zoom first or do you need to--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this joyous occasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my parents' wedding, which was in 1918 in Budapest-- wedding picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are these?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a family picture with me. I'm the little one, of course, in the left-- on the right side actually. And facing from the left, and my brother is back and my parents. And this was sometime in the 1932, '31 in Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Anna, and she was probably, at that time, 19 years old in Budapest. And it was done in 1925. This is a picture about me, my brother my uncle, my aunt's son, and my cousins. These young men was killed in Russia, and she died in Auschwitz. The elementary school--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait a sec.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture made in 1934 after that I finished the third grade elementary in the [HUNGARIAN] School in Budapest. I'm sitting in the second on this side. This picture is the market where my parents had their business. And actually, from the window of our home, which is also on the market square, or [INAUDIBLE], we call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happens if you go straight in, with it locked in so that--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a cemetery of Zenta. And I went back with my mother to find some of her ancestors. We couldn't find, because the thing was so neglected. And this is were her parents are buried. And as you can see, the whole cemetery, in the next picture also, is very neglected, and no one attending it. No one survived. This is the cemetery again. And this, we went back with my mother when she was 70 years old. And as you can see, it was so neglected, we couldn't find anything there. So she found only one of her relatives. No one in that area survived. This is my mother's brother and his family. He died in the '40s, and his children and his wife were taken to Auschwitz, and we never heard anything since then from them. And I will show you another picture of them-- the two youngsters at a little bit older age. This is a little bit more of the two children. It's prior, before the Hungarians went actually in Serbia and occupied this place. And one is called Hugo and the other is Terika. And they were both, of course, killed as I mentioned before. This is the cemetery, the [? Martyrs ?] Cemetery in Budapest, where my father is. Next to it, a memorial place. This is in Rakoskereszturi, which is the Jewish cemetery. As you can see, he was the first who was buried there, but you see now graves behind. And these are indeed people who were killed but somehow were found and were able to identify them. And they brought them back to the Jewish cemetery. He was very buried there in-- in October of 1945. He was killed in December of 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's-- OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you rolling?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is why my mother and I at my wedding, which was in-- took place in Minneapolis in 1969, May. This is my mother in New York City, somewhere in the '80s or '70s. She lived until 86 years of age. And then, we have one more. And this is I'm with my daughter, Rebecca Alexandria, who is our hope for the better future. And she's a college student in Notre Dame, St. Mary's. She wants to be a lawyer so she can sue the doctors. [LAUGHS]
